327: 
328: ## 6. Information Architecture
329: 
330: ### 6.1 Views
331: 
332: Views are shared across roles; access is scoped by AUTH rules (AUTH-1 through AUTH-5).
333: 
334: ```text
335: ├── Landing / Program Overview
336: │   ├── Cohort selector                        [instructor, admin]
337: │   └── Cross-cohort aggregate stats           [instructor, admin]
338: │
339: ├── Cohort Dashboard (per cohort)
340: │   ├── Leaderboard (progress + key metrics)   [all roles]
341: │   ├── Cohort-level aggregate stats           [instructor, admin]
342: │   ├── Flags (behind, excelling, inactive)    [instructor, admin]
343: │   └── Demo Gallery (filterable)              [all roles]
344: │
345: ├── Participant Detail
346: │   │  Participants see their own; instructors
347: │   │  drill down from the cohort dashboard.
348: │   ├── Module Progress Timeline               [all roles]
349: │   ├── Activity Stream                        [all roles]
350: │   ├── Metrics Dashboard (commits, AI usage)  [all roles]
351: │   ├── Commit Detail (history, file changes)  [all roles]
352: │   ├── Proof Artifacts (per module)           [all roles]
353: │   └── Transcript Analytics (opt-in)          [all roles]
354: │
355: ├── Evaluation (instructor-only)
356: │   ├── Survey responses (via Formbricks)      [instructor, admin]
357: │   ├── Exit interview template                [instructor, admin]
358: │   └── Ambassador candidacy assessment        [instructor, admin]
359: │
360: ├── Admin
361: │   ├── Cohort management (create, configure)  [admin]
362: │   └── User management (roles, access)        [admin]
363: │
364: └── Profile / Settings
365:     └── Edit linked accounts (GitHub, Slack)    [all roles]
366: ```
367: 
368: ### 6.2 Data Model (Conceptual)
369: 
370: #### User
371: 
372: A person with access to the tracker — participant, instructor, or admin.
373: 
374: | Attribute | Notes |
375: |-----------|-------|
376: | `id`, `name`, `email`, `auth_provider_id` | Core identity; email from auth provider |
377: | `role` | `participant`, `instructor`, `admin` |
378: | `github_username`, `github_user_id` | Display name collected at onboarding (AUTH-5); numeric ID resolved via GitHub API (immutable) |
379: | `slack_display_name`, `slack_user_id` | Display name collected at onboarding (AUTH-5); user ID resolved via Slack API (immutable) |
380: 
381: #### Cohort
382: 
383: A single run of the Forge program with a defined start, end, and module structure.
384: 
385: | Attribute | Notes |
386: |-----------|-------|
387: | `id`, `name`, `start_date`, `end_date` | Cohort lifecycle |
388: | `status` | `active`, `completed`, `archived` |
389: | `module_config` | Module structure for this cohort |
390: 
391: #### CohortMembership
392: 
393: Associates a participant with a cohort. Supports historical tracking for reassigned participants (repeaters).
394: 
395: | Attribute | Notes |
396: |-----------|-------|
397: | `user_id`, `cohort_id` | FK references; participants only |
398: | `joined_at` | Timestamp; when the participant was assigned |
399: | `left_at` | Timestamp; null while active; set on reassignment or cohort completion |
400: | `is_active` | Boolean; unique constraint: one active membership per participant (COHORT-5) |
401: 
402: > Instructors and admins are not assigned to cohorts — they have visibility across all cohorts via their role (AUTH-2, AUTH-4). Cohort membership is strictly for grouping participants. When a participant is reassigned to a new cohort, the previous membership row is closed (`is_active` = false, `left_at` set) and a new row is created. Historical rows are immutable.
403: 
404: #### ParticipantRepo
405: 
406: Tracks which GitHub repo a participant is using for their cohort.
407: 
408: | Attribute | Notes |
409: |-----------|-------|
410: | `id`, `user_id`, `cohort_id` | FK references |
411: | `github_repo_url` | Participant's forked repo for this cohort |
412: | `tracked_since` | When tracking began |
413: 
414: > Issue state is always derived live from the GitHub API — the tracker stores the repo pointer, not issue data. This avoids dual source-of-truth problems (GIT-1, GIT-2).
415: 
416: #### Module
417: 
418: A unit of the Forge curriculum within a cohort (e.g., Module 1 — Foundations).
419: 
420: | Attribute | Notes |
421: |-----------|-------|
422: | `id`, `cohort_id`, `number`, `name` | Module identity |
423: | `description`, `exit_criteria` | What "done" looks like |
424: 
425: #### ModuleProgress
426: 
427: Records a participant's completion status for a single module.
428: 
429: | Attribute | Notes |
430: |-----------|-------|
431: | `id`, `user_id`, `module_id` | FK references |
432: | `status` | `in_progress`, `complete`, `overridden` |
433: | `completed_at` | Timestamp |
434: | `overridden_by` | FK to User; null unless an instructor adjusted status (replaces boolean flag) |
435: | `override_reason` | Text; required when `overridden_by` is set |
436: 
437: #### ProofArtifact
438: 
439: A link to evidence of work submitted against a module (spec, PR, demo, test output).
440: 
441: | Attribute | Notes |
442: |-----------|-------|
443: | `id`, `user_id`, `module_id` | FK references |
444: | `type` | `spec`, `pr`, `demo`, `test` |
445: | `url`, `submitted_at` | Link to artifact |
446: 
447: #### Demo
448: 
449: A demo submission linking a video and its Slack discussion thread to a module.
450: 
451: | Attribute | Notes |
452: |-----------|-------|
453: | `id`, `user_id`, `module_id` | FK references |
454: | `url` | Video URL |
455: | `slack_thread_url` | Link to Slack discussion thread |
456: | `submitted_at` | Timestamp |
457: 
458: #### ActivityEvent
459: 
460: A single entry in a participant's activity stream (milestone, demo, commit summary, etc.).
461: 
462: | Attribute | Notes |
463: |-----------|-------|
464: | `id`, `user_id`, `cohort_id` | FK references |
465: | `event_type` | `milestone`, `demo`, `commit_summary`, `pr`, `issue_close`, `message` |
466: | `metadata` | JSON; event-specific data |
467: | `timestamp` | When it occurred |
468: 
469: #### MetricSnapshot
470: 
471: Time-series store — one row per metric per participant per period, populated by scheduled integration jobs (GIT-7) and API pulls. Composite metrics (e.g., "12 commits across 3 PRs") are stored as separate rows, not combined.
472: 
473: | Attribute | Notes |
474: |-----------|-------|
475: | `id`, `user_id`, `cohort_id` | FK references |
476: | `metric_type` | See known types below |
477: | `value` | Single numeric value |
478: | `period_start`, `period_end` | Time window for this snapshot |
479: 
480: Known metric types:
481: 
482: | metric_type | Source | Unit |
483: |-------------|--------|------|
484: | `commits_per_day` | GitHub (GIT-7) | count |
485: | `pr_count` | GitHub (MET-1) | count |
486: | `pr_cycle_time` | GitHub (GIT-7) | hours |
487: | `lines_changed` | GitHub (GIT-7) | count |
488: | `code_review_activity` | GitHub (MET-1) | count |
489: | `ai_session_count` | AI tool API (MET-2) | count |
490: | `ai_message_volume` | AI tool API (MET-2) | count |
491: 
492: #### Evaluation
493: 
494: An end-of-program assessment record (survey response, exit interview, or ambassador evaluation).
495: 
496: | Attribute | Notes |
497: |-----------|-------|
498: | `id`, `user_id`, `cohort_id` | FK references |
499: | `type` | `survey`, `interview`, `ambassador` |
500: | `data` | JSON; evaluation content |
501: | `completed_at` | Timestamp |
502: 
503: #### ProgressSuggestion
504: 
505: An AI-generated suggestion that a participant has reached a milestone, pending their confirmation.
506: 
507: | Attribute | Notes |
508: |-----------|-------|
509: | `id`, `user_id`, `module_id` | FK references |
510: | `source_type` | `slack` or `github` |
511: | `source_url` | Link to originating message, PR, or issue |
512: | `suggested_milestone` | What the system thinks was achieved |
513: | `confidence` | Detection confidence score |
514: | `status` | `pending`, `confirmed`, `dismissed` |
515: | `created_at`, `resolved_at` | Lifecycle timestamps |
516: 
517: ## 7. Technical Architecture
518: 
519: ### 7.1 Technology Stack
520: 
521: | Layer | Technology | Rationale |
522: |-------|-----------|-----------|
523: | **Package Manager** | pnpm (pinned via Corepack) | Fast, disk-efficient; lockfile committed; enforced via `packageManager` in `package.json` |
524: | **Frontend** | React + Vite | Lightweight SPA toolchain; fast dev server and builds; no SSR needed for an authenticated internal tool |
525: | **Backend/API** | Supabase (PostgreSQL + Auth + Realtime) | Google OAuth built-in; row-level security; fast to ship |
526: | **Server-Side Logic** | Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) | Webhook receivers, scheduled jobs, LLM calls, Slack bot interactions — no separate backend service needed |
527: | **Auth** | Supabase Auth with Google OAuth (production); email/password auth (local development) | Native Google workspace login; role claims via custom claims or profiles table |
528: | **Database** | Supabase PostgreSQL | Relational model fits the data; RLS for access control |
529: | **Scheduling** | pg_cron + pg_net (Supabase built-in) | Cron jobs in Postgres trigger Edge Functions for scheduled metric pulls (GIT-7) |
530: | **Hosting** | Custom hosting (frontend static deploy) + Supabase (Edge Functions, DB, Auth) | Vite produces static assets deployable to any CDN or web server; no platform lock-in |
531: | **Integrations** | GitHub API, Slack API, AI tool usage API(s), Formbricks API/MCP | Pull metrics and survey data from source systems |
532: 
533: ### 7.2 Integration Points
534: 
535: ```text
536: ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
537: │                     Forge Tracker App                      │
538: │                                                            │
539: │  ┌────────────────┐          ┌───────────────────────────┐ │
540: │  │   Frontend     │          │         Supabase          │ │
541: │  │  (React+Vite)  │────────▶│  ┌──────┐  ┌────────────┐ │ │
542: │  │  Static SPA    │          │  │  DB  │  │ Edge Fns   │ │ │
543: │  └────────────────┘          │  │(RLS) │  │ (Deno)     │ │ │
544: │                              │  └──────┘  └─────┬──────┘ │ │
545: │                              │  ┌──────┐        │        │ │
546: │                              │  │ Auth │  ┌─────┴──────┐ │ │
547: │                              │  └──────┘  │  pg_cron   │ │ │
548: │                              │            └────────────┘ │ │
549: │                              └──────────────┬────────────┘ │
550: └─────────────────────────────────────────────┼──────────────┘
551:                                               │
552:               ┌──────────┬──────────┬─────────┴──┐
553:               │          │          │            │
554:          ┌────▼───┐ ┌────▼───┐ ┌────▼───┐ ┌──────▼─────┐
555:          │ GitHub │ │ Slack  │ │AI Tools│ │ Formbricks │
556:          │  API   │ │  API   │ │  API   │ │  API/MCP   │
557:          └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────────┘
558: 
559:   Webhooks ──▶ Edge Functions (GitHub, Slack callbacks)
560:   Scheduled ──▶ pg_cron triggers Edge Functions (metric pulls)
561:   Client ──▶ Supabase DB directly via RLS (CRUD, realtime)
562: ```
563: 
564: ### 7.3 Key Technical Decisions
565: 
566: 1. **Vite over Next.js.** This is an authenticated internal tool with no public-facing pages — SSR/SSG provides no benefit. Vite is simpler, faster to build, and avoids Next.js complexity (server components, file-based routing opinions). Server-side concerns (webhooks, LLM calls, scheduled jobs) are handled by Supabase Edge Functions rather than Next.js API routes.
567: 2. **Supabase over custom backend.** Reduces time-to-ship significantly. Auth, database, realtime subscriptions, and edge functions in one platform. Edge Functions (Deno) cover all server-side logic without a separate backend service.
568: 3. **pnpm over npm/yarn.** Faster installs, strict dependency resolution, disk-efficient. Pinned via Corepack to enforce consistency across developer machines and CI.
569: 4. **Google OAuth only (initially).** All Forge participants are Liatrio employees; Google workspace login is sufficient. Email/password auth available for local development. Expand auth options when taking to clients.
570: 5. **Row-Level Security (RLS).** Supabase RLS policies enforce that participants see only their own data while instructors see all cohorts. The frontend queries Supabase directly — no API layer needed for CRUD.
571: 6. **Hybrid integration model.** GitHub uses webhooks for real-time PR/issue events (driving progress detection) alongside scheduled pulls for commit metrics. Slack uses the Events API for real-time message ingestion. Formbricks and AI tool usage data remain pull-based. Scheduled jobs use pg_cron triggering Edge Functions. This balances responsiveness for progress detection with simplicity for batch metrics. All event ingestion (webhooks and scheduled pulls) must be idempotent, using source-provided event IDs as deduplication keys to handle retries and overlapping pulls safely.
572: 
573: ## 8. Scope and Phasing
574: 
575: ### Phase 1 — MVP (Skateboard)
576: 
577: **Goal:** Participants can track progress; instructors can see who is where.
578: 
579: - Email/password auth login (Google OAuth deferred to production deployment)
580: - Participant onboarding data collection (AUTH-5: GitHub username, repo URL, Slack username)
581: - Role-based access (participant, instructor, admin)
582: - Cohort and participant management with single-cohort enforcement (COHORT-5)
583: - Module completion marking (participant self-service)
584: - Basic cohort dashboard / leaderboard (module progress)
585: - Per-participant progress timeline
586: - Demo link submission per module
587: 
588: **Target:** Usable by Cohort 2 within 1-2 weeks.
589: 
590: ### Phase 2 — Metrics Integration (Bike)
591: 
592: **Goal:** Surface engagement signals from external tools.
593: 
594: - GitHub integration (commit and PR metrics)
595: - GitHub issue closure tracking for Module 1 deterministic progress (GIT-1 through GIT-3)
596: - Commit pattern metrics for instructor dashboard (GIT-7)
597: - GitHub activity in the activity stream — EOD commit summaries, PR opens/merges, issue closures (ACT-5)
598: - Slack integration (module completion notifications + message ingestion from Forge channels)
599: - Formbricks survey response ingestion
600: - Basic metrics dashboard (commit trends, demo count)
601: - Activity stream with external events
602: - Instructor drill-down view
603: 
604: ### Phase 3 — Evaluation and Analytics (Car)
605: 
606: **Goal:** Support end-of-program evaluation and deeper analytics.
607: 
608: - AI-assisted progress detection (Slack monitoring + semantic analysis + participant prompting)
609: - GitHub PR-based milestone detection with LLM analysis (GIT-4 through GIT-6)
610: - Commit activity pattern detection for instructor flags (GIT-8)
611: - End-of-program survey and exit interview tooling
612: - AI Ambassador candidacy evaluation form
613: - AI tool usage metrics integration
614: - Trend visualization and comparative analytics
615: - Exportable participant summaries
616: 
617: ### Phase 4 — Advanced and Client-Ready (Jet)
618: 
619: **Goal:** Polish for client deployment and advanced analytics.
620: 
621: - AI tool transcript upload and analytics
622: - Deep commit semantic analysis for automatic evidence trails (GIT-9)
623: - Cross-cohort analytics for program managers
624: - White-label / configurable branding for client deployments
625: - Learning path customization (support non-Forge programs)
626: 
627: ## 9. Non-Functional Requirements
628: 
629: | Category | Requirement |
630: |----------|-------------|
631: | **Performance** | Dashboard loads in < 2s for cohorts up to 50 participants |
632: | **Availability** | Standard SaaS availability; no hard SLA for internal tool |
633: | **Security** | Google OAuth; Supabase RLS; stored user data limited to name, email, GitHub username, and Slack ID |
634: | **Privacy** | Transcript upload is opt-in and clearly disclosed |
635: | **Portability** | Deployable to any Supabase instance + any static hosting target (CDN, S3, internal server); no Liatrio-specific hard dependencies |
636: | **Accessibility** | WCAG 2.1 AA for core flows |
637: | **Data Retention** | Retain cohort data for at least 1 year after cohort end date |
638: 
639: ## 10. Open Questions
640: 
641: | # | Question | Owner | Answer |
642: |---|----------|-------|-------|
643: | 1 | What AI tool usage metrics are available via API vs. requiring manual export? | Damien / Robert |  |
644: | 2 | Should the Forge buddy pairing be tracked in this tool? | Damien / Joshua Burns |  |
645: | 3 | What does Robert's AI Ambassador charter look like, and how does evaluation feed into it? | Damien / Robert |  |
646: | 4 | Are there existing AI vendor training resources (e.g., Anthropic's Skilljar) that should integrate? | Damien |  |
647: | 5 | Should Learning 360 data feed into this tool, or are they fully separate? | Team |  |
648: | 6 | What Slack bot permissions are needed for message ingestion? | Damien |  |
649: | 7 | ~~How should the tool handle participants in multiple cohorts (e.g., repeaters or TAs)?~~ | Damien | Resolved: a participant can only belong to one cohort at a time (COHORT-5). Repeaters would be re-assigned to a new cohort; TAs use the Instructor role. |
650: | 8 | What Formbricks surveys does Josh currently run, and can we use the MCP to pull responses into the tracker? | Damien / Josh Guice |  |
651: | 9 | Where will the Vite SPA static assets be hosted? (Cloudflare Pages, S3, internal server, etc.) | Damien |  |
652: | 10 | Which Supabase plan tier? pg_cron and pg_net require the Pro plan; Edge Function invocation limits vary by tier. | Damien |  |
653: 
654: ## 11. Risks and Mitigations
655: 
656: | Risk | Impact | Mitigation |
657: |------|--------|------------|
658: | Integration APIs change or are rate-limited | Metrics data gaps | Use pull-based ingestion with caching; degrade gracefully |
659: | Low participant adoption of self-service tracking | Tool provides no value | Make completion marking lightweight (one click); tie to Slack notifications for social proof |
660: | Scope creep from analytics features | MVP delayed | Strict phasing; Phase 1 has no external integrations |
661: | Privacy concerns with transcript analytics | Participant pushback | Make transcript upload fully opt-in; clear data usage disclosure |
662: | Instructor time to conduct exit interviews | Bottleneck at end of cohort | Pre-populate interview data from tool; provide structured template |
663: | Supabase platform dependency | DB, Auth, Edge Functions, and Realtime all on one provider; outage takes down the entire tool | Accept for MVP; document runbook for Supabase incidents; evaluate self-hosted Supabase or migration path for client deployments (G6) |
664: 
665: ## 12. Appendix
666: 
667: ### A. Forge Module Structure Reference
668: 
669: | Module | Name | Key Deliverables |
670: |--------|------|-----------------|
671: | 1 | AI-Driven Development Foundations | Spec docs, task breakdowns, merged PRs, test output, demo recording |
672: | 2 | AI-Augmented Features & Systems Thinking | Above + architecture doc, retro |
673: | 3 | AI-Accelerated DevOps & Platform Engineering | Above + DevEx tooling demo |
674: | 4 | Autonomous Capstone & Client Enablement | Capstone product, case study, playbook contribution, reflection |
675: 
676: ### B. Proof Artifact Types
677: 
678: - Specification document (markdown)
679: - Task breakdown
680: - Pull request link(s)
681: - Test output / proof of passing acceptance criteria
682: - Demo recording (video link)
683: - Architecture document (Module 2+)
684: - Retrospective / reflection (Module 2+)
685: 
686: ### C. Meeting Participants and Stakeholders
687: 
688: | Name | Role | Relevance |
689: |------|------|-----------|
690: | Damien Storm | Innovation Team / <br />Forge Program Lead | Primary owner; building the tracker; instructing participants |
691: | Joshua Roberts | Forge Instructor / Support | Day-to-day cohort support; TA identification |
692: | Robert | VP of Innovation / <br />AI Ambassador Program Lead | Ambassador charter; usage metrics |
693: | Matt Saliano | Innovation Team | Forge Program support |
694: | Gregg Coppen | Innovation Team | Forge Program support |
695: | Josh Guice | Innovation Team Lead | Forge Program support |

# Forge Program Tracker — Product Requirements Document

**Version:** 0.5.0 (Draft)
**Author:** Damien Storm
**Date:** 2026-03-01
**Status:** Draft — Approved for Phase 1 Build

**Changelog:**

- **0.5.0** — Audit remediation pass: explicit Program Manager → Admin role mapping (C1). CohortMembership lifecycle fields for historical tracking (C2). Stable external IDs for GitHub and Slack with API-based resolution at onboarding (C3). Idempotent event processing requirement on hybrid integration model (H2). ModuleProgress audit trail with overridden_by FK and override_reason (M1). Clarified "email/password auth" terminology throughout (L1). Aligned README hosting and auth with PRD decisions (M5). Google OAuth deferred to production deployment; email/password auth for Phase 1.
- **0.4.0** — Refinement pass: vendor-agnostic AI tooling references throughout (client portability). Added AUTH-5 (onboarding data), COHORT-5 (single-cohort constraint), ACT-5 (EOD commit summaries). Refined DEMO-3 as filterable gallery; removed DEMO-4; codified Slack as demo discussion home. Restructured Section 6 views with role annotations; expanded data model to per-entity sections with descriptions and code-fenced attributes. Updated tech stack for Vite + Supabase Edge Functions + pnpm; custom hosting; redesigned integration diagram. Resolved open question #7.
- **0.3.0** — Added GitHub-based progress detection (GIT-1 through GIT-9) layered across phases: deterministic issue closure tracking in Phase 2, PR-based LLM milestone detection in Phase 3, deep commit semantic analysis in Phase 4. Added ParticipantRepo entity to data model. Updated ProgressSuggestion with source_type field. Shifted to hybrid webhook + pull integration model (Technical Decision #4). Expanded INT-3 for webhook support.
- **0.2.0** — Added design principles on minimizing participant burden (Josh Guice feedback). Softened PROG-3 to optional artifact linking. Added Formbricks integration for surveys (INT-5, EVAL-1). Added AI-assisted progress detection feature (Section 5.9) with Slack monitoring, semantic analysis, and participant prompting. Adjusted phasing: Slack ingestion moved to Phase 2, AI detection added to Phase 3. Added ProgressSuggestion to data model.
- **0.1.0** — Initial draft from planning meeting (Damien, Matt, Gregg).

## 1. Purpose

The Forge Program Tracker is a web application for tracking participant progress, engagement, and outcomes across cohorts of the **Liatrio Forge: Immersive AI Mastery Program**. Today there is no centralized place for participants to mark module completion, for instructors to evaluate progress, or for the team to collect the metrics needed to assess program success and identify AI Ambassador candidates.

This tool fills that gap and, by design, is portable enough to bring to client engagements running similar AI-enablement programs.

79: 
80: ## 6. Minimum Acceptable Baseline Vs Stronger Target State

99: - Local desired branch-governance intent is explicit and semantically strong.

102: ## 8. Repo-Settings Desired-State Evidence Summary

117: ## 9. Live Remote State Summary
118: 
119: - Repo: `liatrio-labs/forge-program-tracker`

129:

132: - Active remote workflows listed by GitHub Actions: only `CI - Tests and Linting`
133: - Local files include `.github/workflows/release.yml` and `.github/workflows/ruleset-sync.yml`, but those workflows are not active on the remote default branch today.
134: - Result: local desired state is ahead of default-branch activation, so docs should distinguish "present on this branch" from "active in the live repo."

136: ## 11. Local Remediation Plan
137: 
138: 1. Rewrite `README.md` so current-state setup reflects the docs-first repo that actually exists today, and move `pnpm`/React/Vite/Supabase guidance into an explicitly labeled future implementation section.
139: 2. Rewrite `AGENTS.md` quality commands to prefer provisioned entrypoints (`pre-commit run --all-files`) and label any direct CLI examples as optional prerequisites rather than assumed commands.

143: 6. Remove stale template comments from `.markdownlint.yaml` and `.github/workflows/release.yml`.
144: 7. Update docs to distinguish local branch scaffolding from live default-branch activation until the bootstrap branch is merged.

146: ## 12. Remote Remediation Plan

149: 2. Once local docs and CODEOWNERS are corrected, merge the bootstrap branch so release and ruleset-sync workflows become active on the default branch.
150: 3. After merge, verify Actions registers `release.yml` and `ruleset-sync.yml` on `main` and confirm required status checks still match the live ruleset.
151: 4. If repo settings are intended to remain as currently configured, codify them locally; if not, audit and reconcile them explicitly before any remote apply step.

161: - Full `pre-commit run --all-files` was intentionally **not** executed because configured fixer hooks would modify repo files, which would violate the read-only audit constraint.
162: 
163: ## 14. Residual Risks And Follow-Ups
164: 
165: - The main readiness risk is **trust drift**: docs and agent instructions currently overstate implementation readiness and active automation.
166: - Invalid or non-resolving CODEOWNERS weakens governance credibility and may break future enforced review expectations.
167: - Direct local `gh api` apply guidance creates unnecessary safety risk in a repo that otherwise models good governance discipline.
168: - Because only CI is active on the remote default branch, release automation should not be described as a current live capability until merged and verified.
169: - If the repo intentionally wants to advertise its planned stack early, that is acceptable only when the wording clearly separates target state from runnable present state.
170: 
171: ## Overall Assessment
172: 
173: This repository is **close to a strong docs-first AI-native baseline**, but it is **not yet fully truthful enough to call ready without caveats**. The strongest parts are local governance scaffolding, CI, and ruleset-as-code parity. The most important gaps are current-state command realism, CODEOWNERS validity, unsafe ruleset apply documentation, and documentation that does not clearly separate branch-local scaffolding from live default-branch activation.
